landing.title: Are you a good person?
landing.label: Examination
landing.subtitle: Ten commandments. Eight questions. One verdict.

resumeDialog.title: Pick up where you left off?

test.counterLabel: died since you opened this page

verdict.deathLineTemplate: people died while you answered. You could be next.
verdict.confessionBoth: A {admitted} — by your own admission. And a {denied} — by your evasions.

questions.text: Have you ever told a lie — even a small one?
questions.commandment: You shall not bear false witness
questions.followUp: What about a 'white lie' to spare someone's feelings?
questions.honestFollowUp: Even one lie. A 'white lie,' an exaggeration, a half-truth — by God's standard, that makes you a liar.

questions.text: Have you ever taken something that wasn't yours — even something small?
questions.followUp: Have you ever kept extra change, or downloaded something you didn't pay for?
questions.honestFollowUp: Regardless of value — a pen, someone's time, credit for work that wasn't yours. The value doesn't matter. The act does.

questions.text: Have you ever looked at someone with lust?
questions.followUp: Jesus said, 'Whoever looks at a woman to lust for her has already committed adultery with her in his heart.' — Matthew 5:28 (NKJV)
questions.honestFollowUp: Jesus said that looking with lust is adultery of the heart. Not by culture's standard — by God's.

questions.text: Have you ever been so angry you wished someone harm?
questions.followUp: Even a flash of hatred — Jesus said, 'Whoever is angry with his brother without a cause shall be in danger of the judgment.' — Matthew 5:22 (NKJV)
questions.honestFollowUp: Jesus equated unjust anger with murder. The hatred in your heart is enough.

questions.text: Have you ever used God's name as a curse word?
questions.commandment: You shall not take the name of the LORD your God in vain
questions.followUp: Even 'OMG' as an exclamation — using the Creator's name carelessly?
questions.honestFollowUp: You used the name of the God who gave you life as a throwaway word. That is blasphemy.

questions.text: Have you ever wanted something that belonged to someone else?
questions.followUp: A friend's lifestyle, someone's relationship, a coworker's promotion?
questions.honestFollowUp: Coveting reveals a heart that says 'what God has given me is not enough.' It's ingratitude made personal.

questions.text: Have you ever put something — money, career, relationships — above God?
questions.commandment: You shall have no other gods before Me
questions.followUp: If you've spent more time on your phone than thinking about your Creator...
questions.honestFollowUp: Anything you love, trust, or serve more than God is an idol. It doesn't have to be a statue.

questions.text: Have you ever failed to honor your parents?
questions.commandment: Honor your father and your mother
questions.followUp: Even in your heart — resentment, dismissiveness, ingratitude?
questions.honestFollowUp: Not just outward obedience — God sees the resentment, the eye-rolls, the dismissiveness in your heart.

verdict.title: Guilty.
verdict.subtitle: By God's perfect standard, none of us are good enough.

grace.scripture: For God so loved the world that He gave His only begotten Son, that whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life.
grace.label: Grace

beats.headline: You're guilty. The fine is eternal.
beats.subtitle: You've broken God's Law — not once, but repeatedly. And the penalty for sin is not a fine you can pay. It's death.

beats.headline: Imagine standing before a just Judge. You can't pay. Justice demands death.
beats.subtitle: A good judge can't let a guilty criminal walk free. And God is the ultimate good Judge. Your guilt must be answered for.

beats.headline: But someone steps into the courtroom and pays your fine in full.
beats.subtitle: Every penny. The judge can now legally dismiss your case — not because he's overlooking justice, but because justice has been satisfied.

beats.headline: Jesus Christ — God in flesh — lived the life you couldn't, died the death you deserved, and rose.
beats.subtitle: Two thousand years ago, the Creator entered His creation. He lived perfectly, took your punishment on the cross, and three days later conquered death itself.

beats.headline: Repent — turn from your sin. Trust in Christ alone. God will forgive you and grant you eternal life.
beats.subtitle: This isn't about a prayer or being religious. It's about turning from sin and placing your trust in the One who paid it all.

invitation.heading: What will you do with this?
invitation.thinkingEncouragement: Take your time. This will be here when you're ready.
invitation.dismissedEncouragement: That's okay. The door stays open.

responses.prayed: I will repent and trust in Christ
responses.thinking: I want to think about it

share.whatsappMessage: Are you a good person? Take this test and find out:
share.telegramMessage: Are you a good person? Take this test and find out:

nextSteps.dismissedReturn: Changed your mind? The reading plan is always open.

trackA.whatHappened:
You acknowledged that you've broken God's Law. And you've made the decision to repent — to turn from your sin — and to place your trust in Jesus Christ alone.

If you've genuinely repented and trusted in the Saviour, God has forgiven every sin you've ever committed and granted you eternal life. Not because you earned it — but because of what Christ did on the cross.

"Therefore, if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation; old things have passed away; behold, all things have become new." — 2 Corinthians 5:17 (NKJV)
trackA.readBody: Start with the Gospel of John — it's the best place to meet Jesus for the first time. Or follow our 7-day reading plan.
trackA.prayBody: Talk to God like a Father. He's listening.
trackA.prayPrompt: God, thank You for forgiving me. Help me to understand what You've done for me. Guide me as I read Your Word. Give me the courage to turn from sin and follow You. In Jesus' name, Amen.
trackA.communityBody: You weren't meant to do this alone. Find a Bible-believing church where you can grow.
trackA.learnBody: Explore the foundations of the faith — who Jesus is, what grace means, why the cross matters.
trackA.shareMessage: I just made a decision that changes everything:

trackB.acknowledgment: That's honest. Here are some things worth thinking about.
reflections: If what you heard today is true, what would that change for you?
Have you ever considered that the discomfort you feel might be your conscience agreeing with God's standard?
What would it take for you to investigate this further?
trackB.readingBody: When you're ready, read John chapter 3. It's a conversation between Jesus and a man who had questions just like yours.
trackB.learnBody: Short, honest explanations of the core questions — who Jesus claimed to be, why it matters, what grace is.
trackB.comeBack: This site will be here whenever you're ready. Bookmark it, or start the reading plan when the time is right.

readingPlan.heading: 7-Day Reading Plan
readingPlan.subtitle: A week in the Gospel of John — meeting Jesus for yourself.
readingPlan.allCompleteBody: You've finished the 7-day plan. But this is just the beginning — keep reading, keep praying, keep growing. The Gospel of John has 21 chapters. Why not keep going?
readingPlan.deeperLink: Explore the foundations of what you've been reading.
readingPlan.resetConfirmBody: This will clear all 7 days of progress. You can start again from Day 1 — nothing else is affected.

days.title: The Beginning
days.keyVerse: And the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we beheld His glory, the glory as of the only begotten of the Father, full of grace and truth.
days.reflection: Before Jesus was born in Bethlehem, He existed eternally as God. He wasn't created — He was the Creator who stepped into His creation. The same God whose Law convicted you is the God who became a man to save you.
days.prayer: God, help me to understand who Jesus really is — not just a good teacher, but the Word made flesh. Open my eyes as I read. Amen.

days.title: Born Again
days.keyVerse: Jesus answered and said to him, 'Most assuredly, I say to you, unless one is born again, he cannot see the kingdom of God.'
days.reflection: Nicodemus was a religious leader — a 'good person' by anyone's standard. But Jesus told him that being good wasn't enough. You need a completely new start. That's what happened when you trusted Christ — you were born again.
days.prayer: Lord, thank You for giving me a new beginning. Help me to live as someone who has been born again — not trying to earn Your love, but resting in it. Amen.

days.title: Living Water
days.keyVerse: Jesus answered and said to her, 'Whoever drinks of this water will thirst again, but whoever drinks of the water that I shall give him will never thirst.'
days.reflection: Jesus met this woman at her lowest — ashamed, alone, avoiding others. He didn't condemn her. He offered her something that would satisfy forever. He meets you the same way — wherever you are, however you feel.
days.prayer: Jesus, I've tried to fill my life with things that don't last. Be my living water. Satisfy the thirst that nothing else can. Amen.

days.title: The Good Shepherd
days.keyVerse: I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd gives His life for the sheep.
days.reflection: A hired hand runs when danger comes. But Jesus — the Good Shepherd — willingly laid down His life. Nobody took it from Him. He chose the cross because He chose you.
days.prayer: Good Shepherd, thank You for not running when it cost You everything. Help me to hear Your voice and follow where You lead. Amen.

days.title: The Way
days.keyVerse: Jesus said to him, 'I am the way, the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through Me.'
days.reflection: This is the most exclusive and the most inclusive claim ever made. Exclusive — there's only one way. Inclusive — anyone can take it. Jesus didn't say 'I'll show you the way.' He said 'I AM the way.'
days.prayer: Lord, in a world of many voices and many paths, give me confidence that You are the way. Help me to trust You even when I don't understand. Amen.

days.title: The Vine
days.keyVerse: I am the vine, you are the branches. He who abides in Me, and I in him, bears much fruit; for without Me you can do nothing.
days.reflection: The Christian life isn't about trying harder. It's about staying connected. A branch doesn't strain to produce fruit — it just stays attached to the vine. Your job is to abide. Stay close. Read. Pray. Trust.
days.prayer: Jesus, teach me what it means to abide in You — not to perform for You, but to remain in Your love and let You work through me. Amen.

days.title: Risen
days.keyVerse: Jesus said to him, 'Thomas, because you have seen Me, you have believed. Blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed.'
days.reflection: Thomas doubted. Jesus didn't reject him — He showed up. If you have doubts, bring them to God. He's not afraid of your questions. The resurrection is the foundation of everything — if Jesus rose, then everything He said is true.
days.prayer: Risen Lord, strengthen my faith. When doubts come, remind me that You are alive and that Your promises are sure. Help me to live boldly for You. Amen.

learn.label: Learn
learn.hubSubtitle: Explore the foundations of the gospel — one topic at a time.
learn.resetConfirmBody: This will clear all topic completions and quiz answers. You can start exploring again from the beginning.

topics.title: Who is Jesus?
topics.subtitle: More than a good teacher.
topics.metaDescription: Who was Jesus of Nazareth? Explore the evidence for His deity, humanity, and what it means for you.

sections.heading: More than a good teacher
sections.body:
Most people are happy to call Jesus a good teacher. But Jesus didn't leave that option open. He claimed to be God — not a god, not godlike, but God Himself. 'I and My Father are one' (John 10:30).

C.S. Lewis put it simply: a man who said the sort of things Jesus said would not be a great moral teacher. He would either be a lunatic, a liar, or He would be exactly who He claimed to be — the Lord.

The people who heard Him understood exactly what He was claiming. They picked up stones to kill Him for blasphemy. You don't stone someone for saying 'be kind to your neighbour.'
sections.scripture: I and My Father are one.

quiz.question: If Jesus claimed to be God, what are the only logical options?
options: He was a great moral teacher
He was a lunatic, a liar, or He is Lord
quiz.reveal: A mere teacher doesn't claim to be God. Either He was delusional, deliberately deceiving, or telling the truth. C.S. Lewis called this the 'Lord, Liar, or Lunatic' trilemma — there is no fourth option.

sections.heading: The Word made flesh
sections.body:
The Gospel of John opens with a staggering claim: 'In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.' And then: 'The Word became flesh and dwelt among us.'

Before Bethlehem, before the manger, before Mary — Jesus existed eternally as God. He wasn't created. He was the Creator who stepped into His own creation. The hands that shaped the universe let themselves be nailed to a cross.
sections.scripture: And the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we beheld His glory, the glory as of the only begotten of the Father, full of grace and truth.

sections.heading: What He did
sections.body:
Jesus lived the only sinless life in human history. He healed the sick, gave sight to the blind, and raised the dead. He had authority over nature, disease, demons, and death itself.

But He didn't come primarily to heal or teach. He came to die. He told His disciples repeatedly that He would be handed over, crucified, and rise on the third day. His death was not an accident or a tragedy — it was the plan from the beginning.
sections.scripture: Who, being in the form of God, did not consider it robbery to be equal with God, but made Himself of no reputation, taking the form of a bondservant, and coming in the likeness of men.

sections.heading: Why it matters to you
sections.body:
If Jesus is who He said He is — God in the flesh — then everything He said carries the weight of divine authority. When He said 'I am the way, the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through Me,' He wasn't offering one option among many. He was stating a fact.

If He rose from the dead (and the evidence is compelling), then His claims are validated. Death could not hold Him. And if death could not hold Him, then His promise of eternal life to all who trust in Him is not wishful thinking — it's the most reliable promise ever made.
sections.scripture: Jesus said to him, 'I am the way, the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through Me.'

quiz.question: If Jesus truly rose from the dead, what does that mean about His claims?
options: They are validated — He is who He said He is
quiz.reveal: The resurrection is the ultimate proof. If death could not hold Him, then His promise of eternal life is the most reliable promise ever made.

topics.title: What is Sin?
topics.subtitle: Why God's standard matters — and why none of us meet it.
topics.metaDescription: What does the Bible mean by 'sin'? Understand God's moral standard and why every person falls short.

sections.heading: God's standard
sections.body:
The Ten Commandments are not suggestions. They are God's moral law — a perfect reflection of His character. They tell us what is right and what is wrong, not based on culture or opinion, but based on the nature of God Himself.

You shall not lie. You shall not steal. You shall not commit adultery. You shall not covet. You shall have no other gods before Me. These aren't arbitrary rules — they reveal what a holy God requires of the beings He created in His image.
sections.scripture: For whoever shall keep the whole law, and yet stumble in one point, he is guilty of all.

quiz.question: According to James 2:10, how many commandments do you need to break to be guilty?
quiz.reveal: Just one. 'Whoever shall keep the whole law, and yet stumble in one point, he is guilty of all.' God's standard is perfection, not improvement.

sections.heading: It's not just murder and theft
sections.body:
Jesus raised the bar impossibly high. He said that anger without cause is the same as murder in God's eyes. That looking at someone with lust is the same as adultery. That hating your brother makes you a murderer at heart.

Sin is not just what you do — it's what you think, what you desire, what you harbour in secret. God doesn't just see your actions. He sees your heart. And by that standard, every single one of us is guilty.
sections.scripture: But I say to you that whoever looks at a woman to lust for her has already committed adultery with her in his heart.

sections.heading: Everyone has sinned
sections.body:
Romans 3:23 does not say 'some have sinned' or 'really bad people have sinned.' It says all. Every human being who has ever lived — except Jesus — has broken God's Law.

This is not about being 'good enough.' It's about being perfect — because that is God's standard. And none of us are. Not the religious leader, not the moral philosopher, not the person who volunteers every weekend. All have sinned.
sections.scripture: For all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God.

quiz.question: According to God's standard, how good do you need to be to enter heaven?
options: Good enough to outweigh your bad
quiz.reveal: Perfect. God's standard isn't 'good enough' — it's moral perfection. And 'all have sinned and fall short.' That's why we need a Saviour.

sections.heading: The consequence
sections.body:
Sin is not a minor infraction. It is cosmic treason against the Creator of the universe. And the penalty is not a slap on the wrist — it is death. Not just physical death, but eternal separation from God.

This is the reality that makes the gospel urgent. If sin had no consequence, there would be no need for a Saviour. But sin does have a consequence. And it is permanent. That is why what comes next — what God did about it — is the best news you will ever hear.
sections.scripture: For the wages of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord.

topics.title: Why the Cross?
topics.subtitle: How justice and mercy meet at Calvary.
topics.metaDescription: Why did Jesus have to die on the cross? Understand how God's justice and love come together in the crucifixion.

sections.heading: The problem
sections.body:
God faces what seems like an impossible dilemma. He is perfectly just — He cannot ignore sin, sweep it under the rug, or pretend it didn't happen. A good judge doesn't let guilty criminals walk free.

But God is also perfectly loving. He does not want anyone to perish. He created you, knows you, and loves you. So how can a just God forgive guilty sinners without compromising His justice?
sections.scripture: He is the Rock, His work is perfect; for all His ways are justice, a God of truth and without injustice; righteous and upright is He.

sections.heading: The courtroom
sections.body:
Imagine you're standing before a judge, guilty of a serious crime. The fine is enormous — far more than you could ever pay. Justice demands payment. You're about to be sentenced.

But then someone walks into the courtroom, opens their wallet, and pays your fine in full. Every penny. The judge can now legally dismiss your case — not because he's overlooking justice, but because justice has been satisfied. The fine has been paid.

That is what happened on the cross.
sections.scripture: But He was wounded for our transgressions, He was bruised for our iniquities; the chastisement for our peace was upon Him, and by His stripes we are healed.

quiz.question: In the courtroom analogy, why can the judge legally let you go?
options: Because He decided to overlook your crimes
Because someone paid your fine in full
Because you promised to be better
quiz.reveal: Justice was satisfied — not ignored. Someone paid the penalty you owed. That's substitutionary atonement: Christ took your punishment so God could legally forgive you.

sections.heading: What happened on the cross
sections.body:
On the cross, Jesus took upon Himself the punishment that every human being deserves. He absorbed the full wrath of God against sin — not His own sin (He had none), but ours.

God 'made Him who knew no sin to be sin for us.' The sinless One became the sin-bearer. He experienced the full weight of divine judgment so that we wouldn't have to. This is not a metaphor. This is substitution — He died in your place.
sections.scripture: For He made Him who knew no sin to be sin for us, that we might become the righteousness of God in Him.

sections.heading: It is finished
sections.body:
Jesus' last words on the cross were not 'I am defeated.' They were 'It is finished.' In Greek, the word is tetelestai — a legal and commercial term meaning 'paid in full.'

The debt is settled. There is nothing you can add to what Christ has done. You cannot earn salvation by being good, going to church, or doing charitable works. Those are good things — but they are not payment. The payment has already been made. All that remains is for you to receive it.
sections.scripture: So when Jesus had received the sour wine, He said, 'It is finished!' And bowing His head, He gave up His spirit.

quiz.question: What does 'It is finished' (tetelestai) mean?
quiz.reveal: Tetelestai was a legal and commercial term meaning 'paid in full.' The debt of sin is settled. There is nothing you can add to what Christ has done.

topics.title: What is Repentance?
topics.subtitle: More than feeling sorry — a complete change of direction.
topics.metaDescription: What does it really mean to repent? Learn the biblical definition of repentance and what it looks like in practice.

sections.heading: Not just feeling sorry
sections.body:
Judas felt sorry for betraying Jesus. He was so filled with regret that he returned the thirty pieces of silver and hanged himself. But the Bible never calls that repentance. It calls it remorse.

Remorse is feeling bad about the consequences of sin. Repentance is feeling grieved about the sin itself — because it offends a holy God. Remorse says 'I wish I hadn't been caught.' Repentance says 'I was wrong, and I want to change.'
sections.scripture: For godly sorrow produces repentance leading to salvation, not to be regretted; but the sorrow of the world produces death.

quiz.question: What is the difference between remorse and repentance?
options: Remorse regrets consequences; repentance grieves the sin itself
quiz.reveal: Judas had remorse — he regretted what happened. But repentance is godly sorrow that leads to change. 'I was wrong, and I want to turn from it.'

sections.heading: Turn and trust
sections.body:
Repentance has two parts. First, you turn FROM sin — not just the sins you're comfortable admitting, but the whole orientation of your life away from God. Second, you turn TO God — placing your trust in Jesus Christ and His finished work on the cross.

It's not about cleaning yourself up before coming to God. You come as you are — broken, guilty, empty-handed. And He receives you. Repentance is not a prerequisite for grace. It is the response to grace.
sections.scripture: Repent therefore and be converted, that your sins may be blotted out, so that times of refreshing may come from the presence of the Lord.

quiz.question: Do you need to clean yourself up before coming to God?
options: Yes — you need to be ready first
No — you come as you are
quiz.reveal: You come as you are — broken, guilty, empty-handed. Repentance is not a prerequisite for grace. It is the response to grace.

sections.heading: What it looks like
sections.body:
Repentance is not perfection. It is a new direction. A repentant person still stumbles, still fails, still sins. But they fall forward. They get back up. They hate what they used to love and love what they used to ignore.

Think of the prodigal son. He didn't clean himself up before going home. He came covered in pig filth, reeking of failure. And his father ran to him, embraced him, and restored him. That is the picture of repentance — coming home, just as you are.
sections.scripture: And he arose and came to his father. But when he was still a great way off, his father saw him and had compassion, and ran and fell on his neck and kissed him.

sections.heading: It's a gift
sections.body:
Here is the most surprising thing about repentance: even the ability to repent is a gift from God. You cannot manufacture it. You cannot work it up through willpower or religious effort.

If you feel a pull toward God right now — a desire to turn, to change, to come home — that is not coming from you. That is God drawing you to Himself. Don't resist it. Ask Him for the gift of repentance, and He will give it.
sections.scripture: When they heard these things they became silent; and they glorified God, saying, 'Then God has also granted to the Gentiles repentance to life.'

topics.title: What Happens When I Die?
topics.subtitle: The question everyone asks — and the Bible's answer.
topics.metaDescription: What happens after death? Explore what the Bible says about eternity, judgment, heaven, hell, and the hope that changes everything.

sections.heading: The appointment
sections.body:
The Bible is blunt about death. It is not a mystery to be avoided or a topic to dance around. 'It is appointed for men to die once, but after this the judgment.' Not if — when. Not maybe — certainly.

Every culture, every religion, every philosophy has tried to answer the question of what comes after death. Reincarnation, annihilation, wishful vagueness. But the Bible gives a clear, unambiguous answer: you die once, and then you face God.
sections.scripture: And as it is appointed for men to die once, but after this the judgment.

sections.heading: Two destinations
sections.body:
Jesus spoke more about hell than anyone else in the Bible. He described it as a place of outer darkness, weeping, and gnashing of teeth — eternal separation from God and everything good.

He also spoke about heaven — the presence of God, where there is no more death, no more pain, no more tears. A place prepared for those who belong to Him.

There is no middle ground. No purgatory, no second chance after death, no cosmic do-over. Two roads, two destinations. And the road you're on is determined by what you do with Jesus.
sections.scripture: And these will go away into everlasting punishment, but the righteous into eternal life.

quiz.question: According to the Bible, is there a middle ground between heaven and hell?
options: Yes — purgatory or a second chance
No — two destinations, no middle ground
It depends on how you lived
quiz.reveal: No middle ground. No purgatory, no second chance after death. Two roads, two destinations — and the road is determined by what you do with Jesus.

sections.heading: The death counter is real
sections.body:
1.8 people die every second. That's not a statistic designed to frighten you — it's a reality designed to wake you up. The death counter on this site isn't a gimmick. It's a mirror.

Every one of those numbers was a person — with a name, a family, hopes, fears. And every one of them has now stepped into eternity. One of those numbers, someday, will be yours. The only question is: are you ready?
sections.scripture: So teach us to number our days, that we may gain a heart of wisdom.

sections.heading: But there is hope
sections.body:
God does not want anyone to perish. He is 'not willing that any should perish but that all should come to repentance.' He made a way — the cross. Jesus bridged the gap between a holy God and sinful humanity.

The question is not whether God has provided a way. He has. The question is whether you will take it. Trust in Jesus Christ. Turn from sin. Receive the gift of eternal life. The counter is still ticking — but it doesn't have to be a countdown to judgment. It can be a countdown to coming home.
sections.scripture: For God so loved the world that He gave His only begotten Son, that whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life.

quiz.question: Has God provided a way to be saved from judgment?
options: Yes — through Jesus Christ and the cross
quiz.reveal: God made a way. The question is not whether He provided it — He has. The question is whether you will take it. Trust in Christ. Turn from sin. Receive eternal life.

privacy.heading: Privacy Policy
privacy.description: How we handle your data on this site.

sections.title: What we collect
sections.body: We use PostHog for anonymous analytics (page views, button clicks, funnel completion) and Sentry for error tracking. Vercel Analytics collects anonymous performance metrics. We do not collect your name, email address, or any personally identifiable information.

sections.title: Cookies and local storage
sections.body: We do not use cookies. Your test progress, reading plan completion, and quiz answers are saved in your browser's local storage. This data never leaves your device and is not sent to any server.

sections.title: Analytics consent
sections.body: When you first visit, we ask whether you consent to analytics tracking. If you decline, PostHog is never loaded and no analytics data is collected. You can reset your consent choice at any time by clearing your browser data for this site.

sections.title: Third-party services
sections.body: PostHog (analytics), Sentry (error monitoring), and Vercel (hosting and performance) may process aggregated, anonymous data on their servers. We do not share any data with advertisers or sell data to third parties.

sections.title: Data retention
sections.body: Analytics data is retained according to PostHog's and Sentry's default retention policies. Local storage data persists until you clear your browser data or reset your progress within the app.

sections.title: Contact
sections.body: If you have questions about this privacy policy, you can reach us through the project's GitHub repository.

terms.heading: Terms of Use
terms.description: The terms that govern your use of this site.

sections.title: Purpose
sections.body: This site is a free educational and evangelistic resource. It presents the Christian gospel through an interactive moral examination, scripture-based teaching, and a guided reading plan.

sections.title: No professional advice
sections.body: The content on this site is not professional counselling, legal, medical, or psychological advice. If you are in crisis or need professional help, please contact a qualified professional or a crisis helpline.

sections.title: External links
sections.body: This site links to third-party resources (Bible reading plans, church directories, ministry websites). We are not responsible for the content, availability, or privacy practices of external sites.

sections.title: Intellectual property
sections.body: Scripture quotations are from publicly available Bible translations and are attributed where cited. The site's design, code, and original content are the property of the project's creators.

sections.title: No warranty
sections.body: This site is provided as-is. We make no guarantees about uptime, accuracy of translations, or completeness of content.

sections.title: Changes
sections.body: We may update these terms at any time. Continued use of the site constitutes acceptance of the updated terms.

about.heading: About
about.description: Who we are and why this site exists.

sections.title: Our mission
sections.body: This site exists to present the gospel of Jesus Christ clearly, honestly, and without compromise. We believe every person will stand before God and give an account of their life. Our goal is to help you consider that reality before it's too late.

sections.title: What we believe
sections.body: We hold to the historic Christian faith as revealed in the Bible. We believe that all people have sinned and fall short of God's glory, that the wages of sin is death, and that God demonstrates His love for us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us. Salvation is by grace alone, through faith alone, in Christ alone.

sections.title: The test
sections.body: The moral examination on this site is based on the Ten Commandments — God's moral standard. It is not designed to condemn, but to show every person their need for a Saviour. As the apostle Paul wrote: 'Through the law we become conscious of our sin' (Romans 3:20).

sections.title: Who built this
sections.body: This site was built by Christians who want to share the good news with as many people as possible. It is not affiliated with any specific church or denomination. It is a free resource and always will be.

footer.livingWatersLink: Are You a Good Person? — Living Waters
footer.scripture: For God so loved the world that He gave His only begotten Son, that whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life.
